articles.title: US: Student visa issuances fell by -36% in summer 2025; OPT uncertainty among factors affecting international student demand
articles.summary: US student visa issuances fell by 36% in summer 2025, with uncertainty surrounding the Optional Practical Training (OPT) programme cited as a key factor affecting international student demand. The US government has renewed its focus on the OPT programme, and former Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem, who was replaced on 5 March, had previously responded favourably to Senator Eric…
articles.insight:
The 36% drop in US student visa issuances during summer 2025—linked partly to OPT uncertainty—suggests growing caution among international applicants, though it remains unclear whether this reflects temporary policy anxiety or a deeper, sustained shift in destination preferences across key source markets.  
Admissions teams should track real-time application and enrolment trends by region and programme level, while closely monitoring US regulatory developments around OPT, visa processing timelines, and comparative messaging from peer destinations to assess evolving competitive positioning.

articles.title: Dozens of international students who won Chevening scholarships were denied visas
articles.summary: The UK Home Office denied visa applications for dozens of international students who had been awarded Chevening scholarships from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan. This decision followed a prior ban on student visas for nationals from these countries.
articles.insight:
This incident signals potential volatility in UK student visa policy for nationals from high-risk or conflict-affected regions, possibly dampening demand for UK study among students from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan—and indirectly affecting perceptions across broader Southeast Asian and West African markets.  
Admissions teams should monitor official visa refusal trends, consult agents in affected countries for on-the-ground sentiment shifts, and assess whether prospective applicants from these regions are increasingly inquiring about alternative destinations like the US, Australia, or New Zealand.

articles.title: Recent Correspondence Between Schmitt and Noem Indicates OPT Reform Still on DHS Agenda
articles.summary: Senator Eric Schmitt (R-Missouri) recently corresponded with DHS Secretary Kristi Noem regarding reform or termination of the Optional Practical Training (OPT) program. In her response, Secretary Noem referenced a Spring 2025 regulatory agenda item on practical training—posted on September 4, 2025—and confirmed it will proceed through the standard Administrative Procedure Act notice-and-comment process.
articles.insight:
This news signals potential future tightening of post-study work pathways in the U.S., which may dampen OPT-dependent markets’ enthusiasm for U.S. study—though actual policy change remains uncertain pending rulemaking, and broader demand drivers like academic quality and cost still dominate student decisions.  
Admissions teams should track the Spring 2025 regulatory timeline closely, assess country-specific OPT reliance in their applicant pools, and prepare balanced messaging about U.S. work opportunities—while avoiding premature assumptions before proposed rules are published and finalized.

articles.title: New Zealand expands post-study work opportunities for international students
articles.summary: New Zealand plans to introduce a new Short Term Graduate Work Visa and extend eligibility for the Post Study Work Visa, with implementation scheduled for late 2026. No specific launch date has been announced, and the changes align with New Zealand’s international education strategy focused on managed growth over the coming years.
articles.insight:
This policy shift signals New Zealand’s intent to remain competitive for international students by enhancing post-study work pathways, potentially influencing student preferences—especially among graduates prioritising skilled migration—though direct demand impacts depend on implementation details and comparative offers from peer destinations.  
Admissions teams should track how New Zealand’s late-2026 rollout aligns with evolving source-market perceptions and peer-country visa reforms, while reviewing messaging around graduate outcomes to ensure clarity on current versus future opportunities.

articles.title: UK denies visas to some winners of Chevening, its own most prestigious scholarship
articles.summary: The UK government has denied student visas to dozens of Chevening Scholarship winners, its most prestigious international scholarship program. This occurred after the UK imposed a blanket ban on student visas from four countries affected by war.
articles.insight:
This incident signals potential reputational and trust risks for the UK’s international education brand, possibly dampening demand from conflict-affected regions—even if broader visa policy shifts remain unclear, it may amplify perceptions of unpredictability in UK immigration pathways.  
Admissions teams should monitor application trends from high-risk source countries, track official visa refusal data by nationality, and assess whether partner agents or universities report heightened student concerns about UK visa reliability versus alternatives like Canada or Australia.

articles.title: DOS Rulemaking on Subpart C of the Exchange Visitor Regulations
articles.summary: On March 10, 2026, the U.S. Department of State submitted a proposed rule titled “Exchange Visitor Rules: Records” to the Office of Management and Budget for regulatory review. The proposal would replace existing provisions at 22 CFR 62.43 (Extension of program) and 22 CFR 62.45 (Reinstatement to valid program status) to align the Exchange Visitor Regulations with the current functionality of the Student and Exchange Visitor Information System (SEVIS).
articles.insight:
This regulatory update reflects ongoing U.S. administrative efforts to modernize exchange visitor compliance infrastructure, which may indirectly influence international student perceptions of visa processing reliability and program flexibility—though no direct impact on undergraduate or graduate degree-seeking students is indicated at this stage.  
Admissions teams should track whether the final rule introduces new documentation expectations for J-1 affiliated programs (e.g., internships, research scholars) and assess implications for partner institutions or pathway providers that rely on SEVIS-aligned processes in key source markets.

articles.title: SEVP Page - Beware of DHS Imposters: Protecting Foreign Students from Scams
articles.summary: A February 19, 2026 SEVP resource titled “Beware of DHS Imposters: Protecting Foreign Students from Scams” warns international students about scammers impersonating U.S. Department of Homeland Security officials to solicit money or personal information. The guidance instructs Designated School Officials to remind students that government agencies never demand payment by phone, to share scam-prevention information, and to direct affected students to report suspicious contacts and seek assistance from their school.
articles.insight:
This advisory signals growing regulatory attention to fraud targeting international students, potentially heightening anxiety among prospective applicants—especially in high-scam-risk markets—though direct impacts on demand or visa policy remain unconfirmed and likely modest in the near term.  
Admissions teams should monitor regional scam-reporting trends and student support queries for early signs of eroded trust, and consider integrating official fraud-prevention messaging into pre-enrollment communications—particularly for students from countries with elevated phishing or impersonation incidents.

articles.title: Top French business school launches Mumbai hub in India push
articles.summary: ESSEC Business School, a French grande école, has launched its third international hub in Mumbai as part of its strategy to establish a long-term presence in India. This initiative follows French President Emmanuel Macron’s recent visit to India, which aimed to strengthen bilateral ties, especially in AI and technology.
articles.insight:
ESSEC’s Mumbai hub signals growing institutional confidence in India as a strategic education market, potentially reflecting rising demand for globally recognised degrees with local access—though it doesn’t directly indicate shifting student preferences toward France over traditional Anglophone destinations.  
Admissions teams should monitor whether such hubs catalyse increased Indian student applications to French institutions, track bilateral policy developments (e.g., visa facilitation or AI-focused scholarships), and assess if similar moves by other European schools could reshape competitive positioning in key Indian cities.

articles.title: IIM Ahmedabad opens global GMP admissions with Dubai immersion and leadership training for executives
articles.summary: IIM Ahmedabad has opened admissions for the 24th cohort of its eight-month General Management Programme, targeting mid-to-senior professionals from the Middle East, Europe, and South Asia. The KHDA-approved programme features blended learning, case-based pedagogy, and campus immersions in Dubai and Ahmedabad, with a focus on leadership, digital transformation, and strategic decision-making.
articles.insight:
This initiative signals growing demand for shorter, regionally anchored executive education—potentially reflecting shifting preferences among mid-career professionals in key markets like MENA and South Asia, though it’s unclear whether this directly competes with or complements traditional full-degree international study.  
Admissions teams should monitor enrolment patterns in similar blended, location-flexible programmes across Dubai, Singapore, and London, and assess whether alumni from such offerings later pursue advanced degrees in their regions or Western institutions.

articles.title: Webinar for Students Transitioning from F-1 to H-1B
articles.summary: The International Student Resource Center (ISRC) and Interstride are hosting a webinar titled “F-1 Student to H-1B Worker: Process and Planning in Turbulent Times” on February 12, 2026, at 1:00 PM ET. The event features a panel of immigration attorneys and international education professionals who will explain the F-1 to H-1B transition pathway and address recent immigration policy and regulatory changes affecting the process.
articles.insight:
This webinar reflects growing institutional awareness of post-study work pathway uncertainty, potentially signalling heightened student concern about H-1B viability—though direct impact on enrolment decisions remains unclear amid broader economic and policy volatility.  
Admissions teams should track H-1B lottery trends, USCIS processing delays, and source-market sentiment shifts, while reviewing how their career support messaging aligns with evolving U.S. immigration realities for prospective applicants.
